title: What Is a Control?
subtitle: The everyday safeguards that keep things honest

hook.metaphor: You already run dozens of internal controls at home: you lock the door, you check the change from a shopkeeper, two signatures are needed on a big family decision. A control is just a deliberate safeguard that makes a bad outcome less likely. Scale that up to an organisation and you have the discipline of internal controls.
hook.scenario: A small charity lets one trusted volunteer receive the cash, record it, and bank it — alone. For years it's fine. Then ₹8 lakh quietly goes missing. No villain was hired; a missing control created the opportunity. What single safeguard would have changed everything?

learn: An **internal control is a process or safeguard that gives *reasonable assurance* you'll meet your objectives** — accurate reporting, smooth operations, and compliance with the rules. That phrase 'reasonable assurance' matters: controls reduce risk, they don't promise perfection. The goal is to make failure unlikely and detectable, not impossible.

Controls serve three broad objectives (straight from the **COSO** framework you met in Credit Risk): **operations** (things run effectively), **reporting** (the numbers are trustworthy), and **compliance** (you obey the law). A good control usually protects at least one of these.

Crucially, controls aren't about assuming people are crooks — they're about removing **opportunity** and catching honest mistakes. Our charity's loss wasn't really about one bad volunteer; it was that one person controlled the whole cash cycle with nobody checking. The fix (splitting those duties) protects good people as much as it deters bad ones.

And controls cost something — time, friction, money. So you don't control everything to the maximum; you focus the strongest controls where the **risk** is greatest. Internal controls are the operational answer to the question every risk discipline asks: 'we've found the risk — now what concretely stops it?'
bridge: So at work, you can spot a missing control by asking: *where could one person, one mistake, or one gap cause real harm with nobody noticing?* That's where a safeguard belongs. And you can keep expectations honest — controls give 'reasonable assurance', so the aim is to make problems unlikely and visible, not to chase an impossible zero.

interactions.title: The charity's missing safeguard
interactions.prompt: One volunteer received, recorded, and banked all the cash alone, and money vanished. The key fix?

options.label: Only ever hire people you completely trust.
options.feedback: Trust isn't a control — it's a hope. Even honest people make mistakes, and concentrating the whole cash cycle in one pair of hands is the real weakness, regardless of character.

options.label: Split the duties so no one person controls the whole cash cycle.
options.feedback: Exactly — segregation of duties. When receiving, recording, and banking are separate people, theft or error needs collusion or gets caught. The opportunity disappears.

options.label: Accept that some loss is just inevitable.
options.feedback: This loss was preventable with one basic control. 'Inevitable' is what people say when they haven't designed the safeguard that was sitting right there.

interactions.title: Which objective does it protect?
interactions.prompt: Sort each control by the COSO objective it mainly serves.

items.label: A nightly reconciliation so the accounts are accurate
A backup process so the service keeps running
A KYC check required before onboarding
Sign-off that financial statements are correct
A privacy consent step required by law

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “An internal control is just a deliberate safeguard — like locks or a second signature — that gives reasonable assurance things go right, by removing the opportunity for harm and catching honest mistakes.”

options: A guarantee that nothing can ever go wrong
Reasonable assurance that objectives are met
checkpoint.explain: Controls reduce risk to make failure unlikely and detectable — 'reasonable assurance', not perfection.

checkpoint.q: The three broad objectives controls protect (per COSO) are…
checkpoint.explain: Effective operations, trustworthy reporting, and compliance with laws.

options: Removing opportunity and catching honest mistakes
Slowing everything down for its own sake
checkpoint.explain: They protect good people and deter bad ones by closing gaps where harm could go unnoticed.
